request.question: United 300 and 300 were both made in what country?

title: The Biggest Loser
Foxburg Country Club
University of Nebraska Cornhusker Marching Band
Transportation in Canada
Human trafficking in Uganda
United 300
300 (film)
North Country Community College
Health in Morocco
Maynard carbine
sentences: The Biggest Loser is a reality television show which started in the United States in 2004.
The show centers on overweight and obese contestants attempting to lose the most weight and to fight for a cash prize.
There are different variations of "The Biggest Loser" around the world.
Each country has made its own adaptation to the show; however, the contestants always have the same goal: to lose the highest percentage of weight (or most weight) to become the Biggest Loser.
There is no minimum or maximum weight limit for the show but most males tend to weigh over or near 300 lb (136 kg).
Females tend to weigh over or near 200 lb (91 kg).
Foxburg Country Club, established in 1887, is the oldest golf course in continuous use in the United States.
It is located in Foxburg, Clarion County, Pennsylvania, United States of America, approximately 55 mi north of Pittsburgh on a hill rising about 300 feet above the Allegheny River.
The course was listed in 2007 as Foxburg Country Club and Golf Course on the National Register of Historic Places.
The clubhouse contains the American Golf Hall of Fame.
The University of Nebraska Cornhusker Marching Band (also known as the Marching Red or The Pride of All Nebraska) is the marching band of the University of Nebraska.
It performs at all home football games.
Founded in 1879, the Marching Red is one of the oldest and best-known collegiate marching bands in the United States.
The band consists of 300 students from over 60 different academic majors from across the campus.
Thanks to the success of the Husker football program, the Marching Red is also one of the most traveled bands in the country having participated in all of the major bowl games.

They have appeared multiple times at the Rose, Fiesta, Orange, Sugar, Cotton, Sun, and Alamo Bowls, and have made single appearances at the Bluebonnet, Liberty, Holiday, Citrus, Independence and Gator Bowls.
On November 19, 2011, the Cornhusker Marching Band performed at Michigan Stadium in front of the largest audience ever to watch the band.

Transportation in Canada, the world's second-largest country in total area, is dedicated to having an efficient, high-capacity multimodal transport spanning often vast distances between natural resource extraction sites, agricultural and urban areas.

Canada's transportation system includes more than 1400000 km of roads, 10 major international airports, 300 smaller airports, 72093 km of functioning railway track, and more than 300 commercial ports and harbours that provide access to the Pacific, Atlantic and Arctic oceans as well as the Great Lakes and the St. Lawrence Seaway.
In 2005, the transportation sector made up 4.2% of Canada's GDP, compared to 3.7% for Canada's mining and oil and gas extraction industries.
Uganda is a source and destination country for men, women, and children trafficked for the purposes of forced labor and sexual exploitation.
Ugandan children are trafficked within the country, as well as to Canada, Egypt, the United Arab Emirates, and Saudi Arabia for forced labor and commercial sexual exploitation.
Karamojong women and children are sold in cattle markets or by intermediaries and forced into situations of domestic servitude, sexual exploitation, herding, and begging.

Security companies in Kampala recruit Ugandans to serve as security guards in Iraq where, at times, their travel documents and pay have reportedly been withheld as a means to prevent their departure; these cases may constitute trafficking.
Pakistani, Indian, and Chinese workers are reportedly trafficked to Uganda, and Indian networks traffic Indian children to the country for sexual exploitation.
Children from the Democratic Republic of the Congo (D.R.C.), Rwanda, and Burundi are trafficked to Uganda for agricultural labor and commercial sexual exploitation.

Until August 2006, the terrorist rebel organization, Lord’s Resistance Army (LRA), abducted children and adults in northern Uganda to serve as soldiers, sex slaves, and porters; while no further abductions of Ugandan children have been reported, at least 300 additional people, mostly children, were abducted during the reporting period in the Central African Republic and the D.R.C.
The Government of Uganda does not fully comply with the minimum standards for the elimination of trafficking; however, it is making significant efforts to do so.
United 300 is an American short film that parodies "United 93" (2006) and "300" (2007).
It won the MTV Movie Spoof Award at the MTV Movie Awards 2007.
The short was created by Andy Signore along with some of his friends.
During his speech, he said, roughly, "The film was not making fun of a tragedy; it was a tribute to those who stood up against tyranny".
300 is a 2006 American epic war film based on the 1998 comic series "300" by Frank Miller and Lynn Varley.
Both are fictionalized retellings of the Battle of Thermopylae within the Persian Wars.
The film was directed by Zack Snyder, while Miller served as executive producer and consultant.
It was filmed mostly with a super-imposition chroma key technique, to help replicate the imagery of the original comic book.
North Country Community College is a two-year college of the State University of New York located in Upstate New York.
Founded in 1967, the main campus is in Saranac Lake, New York, and serves Franklin and Essex Counties; enrollment is approximately 1500 students.
It employs 143 permanent and 300 adjunct faculty.
North Country is the only public college located within the Adirondack Park.
The College, located in New York's North Country Region, has additional locations in Malone and Ticonderoga.
In August 2010, North Country Community College was ranked Best Community College in New York and 22nd among the Best Community Colleges in the United States by "Washington Monthly".

The college has also been ranked one of the Top 50 fastest growing community colleges in the United States in "Community College Week Magazine", 101 Best for Vets Colleges in the United States by "Military Times" and a Top 15% Military Friendly Colleges in the U.S., by gijobs.com.
Morocco became an independent country in 1956.
At that time there were only 400 private practitioners and 300 public health physicians in the entire country.
By 1992, the government had thoroughly improved their health care service and quality.
Health care was made available to over 70% of the population.
Programs and courses to teach health and hygiene have been introduced to inform parents and children on how to correctly care for their own and their families' health.
The Maynard carbine was a breech-loaded carbine used by cavalry in the American Civil War.
The First Model was manufactured between 1858 and 1859.
About 5,000 were made.
In United States service it was distributed to the 9th Pennsylvania and 1st Wisconsin cavalry regiments, United States Marines aboard the USS "Saratoga" and the United States Revenue Cutter Service.
About 3,000 Maynard carbines were in Confederate hands during the war; 5,000 in .35 caliber were purchased by Florida, 650 in .50 by Georgia, and 325 in .50 and 300 in .35 by Mississippi.
Around 800 were purchased by militias in South Carolina and Louisiana.
The Second Model or Model 1863 was manufactured between 1863 and 1865.
Over 20,000 were made.
This model lacked the tape primer and stock patchbox, and was used by the 9th and 11th Indiana cavalry regiments and 11th Regiment Tennessee Volunteer Cavalry, among others.
The Maynard had a good reputation for long-range accuracy, and Confederate sharpshooters made extensive use of it, especially during the Siege of Charleston.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Disambiguation for short/ambiguous title tokens:
- If an anchor in summary_1 is a short or potentially ambiguous title token, do not automatically append an entertainment-domain keyword. Only append a plain entertainment disambiguator keyword (for example: film movie album song novel author director) when the original question itself contains explicit entertainment-role words (e.g., film, movie, album, song, directed, author, novella) or when the question explicitly lists multiple title-like tokens. In those cases, include the visible anchor(s) from summary_1 plus the appropriate entertainment keyword to bias retrieval. If the question lacks entertainment cues and does not list multiple titles, do not guess or add an entertainment disambiguator—keep the query anchored to the visible tokens only.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- When the context contains a concise bridge claim about a named entity and at least one passage provides an explicit, identity-aligned statement linking that same named entity (or a clearly indicated alias) to the asserted relation, append exactly one brief corroborating sentence that paraphrases that passage's linking fact and references the corroborating passage. Perform an explicit identity-alignment check (alias or explicit linking); do not treat mere name co-occurrence or a passage that merely mentions the name as sufficient. Do not append this corroboration if the `context` already contains the identical corroborating fact verbatim. Keep this addition short and keep the overall summary compact.
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.
- When the summaries contain both a short single-token alias and, in the later integrated summary (summary_2), a contiguous multi-token full-name literal that explicitly identifies or is explicitly linked to the same person (for example via an identity link or biographical framing in summary_2), prefer and emit that multi-token full-name literal exactly as it appears in summary_2. Do not prefer the longer form unless summary_2 provides an explicit identity link tying the alias to the full-name literal; if no such explicit link is present, follow the base identity-link rules and do not force selection of the longer form.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

response.hop1_query: United 300 and 300 were both made in what country?
hop1_docs: Hansen High School | Hansen High School is a high school in Hansen, Idaho.  They were the 2003 state track division 1A champions.They went to 2009 state track in the 300 hurdles, and 800m.  Their rivals are the Murtaugh Red Devils.  Hansen high school has about 300+ students.  It offers athletic programs for both girls and boys such as football, volleyball, basketball, cross country, and track.

Fortschritt ZT 300 | ZT 300 is a series of 20-kN-tractors produced from 1967 to 1984 by the VEB Traktorenwerk Schönebeck.  It succeeded the "RS14 Famulus" series and unlike the Famulus, the ZT 300 series was sold under the brand name Fortschritt.  "ZT 300" stands for the ZT 300 series tractors as well as the initial tractor Fortschritt ZT 300.  In total, 72.382 tractors were made.  The most built model was the ZT 303 introduced in 1972 with automatic all-wheel-drive; in the early 1980s it would cost 81.000 Mark.  Starting in 1983, the ZT 300 series was succeeded by the ZT 320.

Farman F.300 | The Farman F.300 and F.310 were airliners built in France in the early 1930s.  Built to the same general trimotor layout popular with manufacturers of the time, they were high-wing strut braced monoplanes with fixed tailskid undercarriage.  The cockpit and passenger compartment were fully enclosed.  Most went to equip Farman's own airline, whose twelve F.300 variants made up half its fleet in 1931.

KTM 300 | The KTM 300 is a series of 2-stroke enduro off-road motorcycles made by KTM.  They are the Penton-inspired 1984 300 GS, the 300 DMX, the dirt only 300 MXC, European road legal 300 EXC and their current US counterparts the 300 XC (close-ratio) and 300 XC-W (wide-ratio gear box) respectively.  The KTM 300 is designed for difficult off-road conditions.  The 300 EXC and the 300 XC-W have a small headlight, speedometer, tail-light and somewhat softer linkless PDS suspension.  Starting in 2009 all 300's came with electric start.

Northern 300 | The Northern 300 was a NASCAR Winston Cup Series stock car race held in 1958, 1959, and from 1967 to 1972 at Trenton Speedway in Trenton, New Jersey.  The debut race in 1958 was 500 laps and 500 mi in length, which was cut down to 150 laps, 150 mi for 1959.  The 1967 and 1968 races were both 300 laps, covering 300 mi .  The track was lengthened from 1 mile to 1.5 miles for 1969, and the final four races were cut down from 300 to 200 laps, while still covering a 300-mile distance.  A race in Trenton was on the 1973 schedule but was rained out on race day and ultimately cancelled.  A 1974 race was on the Winston Cup schedule before the season, but was replaced by the Purolator 500 at Pocono Raceway.  Richard Petty was the only driver to win more than once at Trenton, winning three times.

Mervyn Cawston | Mervyn Cawston (born 4 February 1952) is an English former professional footballer who played as a goalkeeper.  Active in both England and the United States, Cawston made over 300 career appearances.

Morane-Saulnier MS.300 | The Morane-Saulnier MS.300 and MS.301 were French parasol wing introductory trainer aircraft, first flown in 1930.  They differed only in engine type.  Neither reached production but were developed into two similar trainers, the MS.230 and MS.315, which were made in large numbers.
response.summary_1: Evidence: the passages state "The Farman F.300 and F.310 were airliners built in France in the early 1930s" and "The Morane‑Saulnier MS.300 ... were French parasol wing introductory trainer aircraft." Missing/ambiguous link: none of the provided passages mention "United 300" or specify which two "300" items the question means. Retrieval direction: fetch the page(s) for "United 300" and the relevant "300" disambiguation entries (or the specific "300" article the question refers to) to identify those two named items and confirm their country of manufacture. Use the quoted anchors "built in France" and "French ... trainer aircraft" when matching candidate "300" articles.
hop2_docs: Community Unit School District 300 (disambiguation) | Community Unit School District 300 (disambiguation)
300 (disambiguation) | 300 is a year, the year 300 CE.  300 may also refer to:

No. 300 Polish Bomber Squadron | No. 300 (Polish) "Land of Masovia" Bomber Squadron (Polish: "300 Dywizjon Bombowy "Ziemi Mazowieckiej"" ) was a Polish World War II bomber unit.  It was fighting alongside the Royal Air Force and operated from airfields in the United Kingdom.

LR-300 | The LR 300 is an American rifle and conversion kit designed by gunsmith Allan Zitta and manufactured by Z-M Weapons.  The "LR" stands for "Light Rifle" and 300 is for 300 meters, the effective range of the rifle with a standard 55-grain FMJ bullet.  It is based on the AR-15 and is broadly similar to the United States service rifle, the M16.
Alsco 300 | The Alsco 300 is a NASCAR Xfinity Series race held at Kentucky Speedway in Sparta, Kentucky, United States.  The distance of the race is 300 miles (482.803 km).

.300 Norma Magnum | The .300 Norma Magnum is a cartridge that has begun to gain popularity in the long range shooting community.  The .300 Norma Magnum is a .338 Norma Magnum case necked down to .308 caliber.  This cartridge was named .300 Norma Magnum to differentiate it from the .308 Norma Magnum designed in 1960.  The United States Special Operations Command (SOCOM) has selected .300 Norma Magnum as their new Advanced Sniper Rifle Cartridge (2016).

KTM 300 | The KTM 300 is a series of 2-stroke enduro off-road motorcycles made by KTM.  They are the Penton-inspired 1984 300 GS, the 300 DMX, the dirt only 300 MXC, European road legal 300 EXC and their current US counterparts the 300 XC (close-ratio) and 300 XC-W (wide-ratio gear box) respectively.  The KTM 300 is designed for difficult off-road conditions.  The 300 EXC and the 300 XC-W have a small headlight, speedometer, tail-light and somewhat softer linkless PDS suspension.  Starting in 2009 all 300's came with electric start.
response.summary_2: The two 300-series aircraft cited in the context (Farman F.300/F.310 and Morane‑Saulnier MS.300) were built in France. The retrieved passages do not identify or link an entity called "United 300," so whether "United 300" refers to one of these or was also made in France is not confirmed by the search results.
